title: Risk, Security & Compliance
description: How risk management, security architecture, and compliance frameworks must evolve when AI agents become autonomous participants in enterprise systems.

traditional.summary: Traditional EA provides a structured risk management technique integrated throughout the development cycle.
traditional.body_html:
Risk Category

Architecture risk — the risk that architecture decisions produce outcomes that fail to meet enterprise objectives or introduce unacceptable exposure.

Assessment

A five-stage process: risk classification (categorising by area), risk identification (workshops and analysis), risk assessment (effect × frequency matrix producing risk ratings), risk mitigation (countermeasures and contingency plans), and risk monitoring (tracking residual risk through implementation).

Controls

Risk owners assigned for each identified risk. Countermeasures defined with contingency plans. Residual risk tracked through Implementation Governance and Change Management. Risk management embedded as an Development-integrated activity, not a separate stream.

questions.text: To what extent has your organisation extended its risk register with AI-specific risk categories?

levels.1: Risk register contains only traditional IT and business risks; AI risks are not captured
levels.2: A few AI risk categories have been added informally but without structured taxonomy or ownership
levels.3: Formal AI risk categories (hallucination, cascade failure, prompt injection, scope violation) are defined in the risk register with assigned owners
levels.4: AI risk categories are systematically assessed using scenario-based methods and reviewed regularly by architecture governance
levels.5: Probabilistic risk models continuously assess AI risks; risk register is dynamically updated based on observed failure patterns and near-misses

questions.text: How does your organisation assess and quantify risks from non-deterministic AI systems?

levels.1: Traditional effect-by-frequency matrices are used without adaptation for AI systems
levels.2: The limitations of traditional matrices for AI risk are acknowledged; ad hoc workarounds are used
levels.3: Scenario-based risk assessments are conducted for major AI deployments, supplementing matrix-based approaches
levels.4: Probabilistic risk assessment methods are applied systematically to AI systems, including multi-agent interaction modelling
levels.5: Predictive risk models anticipate emerging risks from agent interaction patterns before they manifest; assessments are continuously validated against actual failure data

actions.text: Add AI-specific risk categories to your enterprise risk register: agent cascade failure, model hallucination, prompt injection, data poisoning, agent scope violation, and feedback loop risk. Assign a risk owner for each.
Pilot scenario-based risk assessment for your highest-risk AI deployment, modelling at least three specific failure scenarios with estimated likelihood and business impact.
Establish a standing AI risk agenda item in architecture governance, with automated reporting on agent performance anomalies and risk threshold breaches.

changes.summary: Traditional risk matrices <mark>cannot score non-deterministic AI failures</mark> — an agent's risk profile changes with every prompt.
key_shifts: New risk categories required: cascade failure, hallucination, emergent behaviour, feedback loops
Scenario-based assessment supplements the matrix — frequency is unknowable for non-deterministic systems
Continuous monitoring replaces periodic review; agent risk profiles shift at runtime
Risk owners must understand AI failure modes, not just business process failures
what_breaks: Static risk matrices produce meaningless scores for systems whose failure probability depends on runtime context
Risk registers that treat AI as standard IT miss entire categories of agent-specific failure
Spreadsheet-based risk management cannot handle the velocity of AI risk profile changes
Periodic risk reviews discover agent failures months after business damage has occurred
what_survives: The five-stage risk process (classify, identify, assess, mitigate, monitor) remains structurally sound
Risk ownership and accountability principles apply directly to AI systems
Countermeasure and contingency planning logic transfers to agent containment strategies
Development-integrated risk management is more important than ever with agent-augmented architecture

future_state.summary: <mark>Scenario-driven, continuously monitored risk management</mark> where AI-specific categories and runtime telemetry replace static matrices.
future_state.description: Risk registers include agent-specific categories with scenario-based assessment replacing fixed-probability scoring. Continuous monitoring feeds runtime risk signals into governance, and risk owners are trained on AI failure modes.
key_practices: Add agent-specific risk categories to the enterprise register: cascade failure, hallucination, prompt injection, scope violation
Pilot scenario-based assessment on the highest-exposure AI deployment before scaling
Establish automated risk threshold monitoring with governance escalation for agent anomalies
Train risk owners on AI failure modes so they can evaluate agent risks without relying solely on AI teams

examples.relevance: Sompo's $60M AI-driven profit improvement introduced risk categories that traditional frameworks cannot score — model drift, cascade failure between agents, and emergent pricing behaviour.
AIG's automated E&S underwriting with 5x output per underwriter requires risk frameworks that assess AI decision quality, not just process compliance.

traditional.summary: Traditional EA treats security as a cross-cutting concern spanning all architecture domains and development phases.
traditional.body_html:
Risk Category

Enterprise security risk — threats to the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of enterprise systems and data from external attackers, insider threats, and system vulnerabilities.

Assessment

The TSG provides phase-by-phase guidance for incorporating security into each development phase. Threats are identified, controls are defined, and compliance is verified. Security maps to industry security frameworks and standards.

Controls

Security designed into every architecture component as a cross-cutting property, not a layer added afterwards. Defence-in-depth with access controls, encryption, monitoring, and incident response.

questions.text: Has your organisation developed an AI-specific threat model that covers prompt injection, model extraction, data poisoning, and adversarial attacks?

levels.1: No AI-specific threat modelling; AI systems are secured using standard application security measures only
levels.2: AI-specific threats are informally acknowledged but not systematically analysed or documented
levels.3: A comprehensive AI threat model exists with identified attack vectors and mapped controls for each
levels.4: AI security monitoring is integrated into the enterprise security operations centre with automated detection for prompt injection and model behaviour anomalies
levels.5: Adaptive AI security with automated threat detection and response; security properties verified continuously; AI security integrated into CI/CD as automated gates

questions.text: To what extent do your AI agents operate under Zero Trust principles with per-session authentication and minimum-privilege access?

levels.1: AI agents have standing access to enterprise systems with broad permissions
levels.2: Some access restrictions exist but agents generally have persistent credentials and wide-scope permissions
levels.3: Zero Trust applied to agent access: per-session authentication, time-limited tokens, minimum required permissions for all production agents
levels.4: Continuous authorisation evaluation for all agents; agent-to-agent communication authenticated at every interaction; automated privilege escalation detection
levels.5: Dynamic, context-aware access management where agent permissions adapt based on task requirements, risk level, and real-time trust assessment

questions.text: How mature is your organisation's ability to detect and respond to AI-specific security incidents?

levels.1: No AI-specific incident response capability; AI incidents handled as general IT security events
levels.2: Some awareness of AI-specific attack vectors among security teams; no dedicated playbooks
levels.3: AI-specific incident response playbooks defined for major threat categories; security team trained on AI attack vectors
levels.4: Automated detection of AI-specific attacks integrated into SOC operations; real-time model behaviour monitoring for extraction and poisoning indicators
levels.5: Proactive threat hunting for AI-specific attack patterns; automated containment and recovery; continuous red-teaming of AI systems

actions.text: Develop an AI threat model covering prompt injection (direct and indirect), model extraction, data poisoning, adversarial inputs, and agent impersonation. Map each threat to existing and required controls.
Apply Zero Trust principles to all deployed AI agents: per-session authentication, minimum-privilege access, time-limited tokens, and authenticated agent-to-agent communication.
Create AI-specific incident response playbooks and integrate AI security monitoring into enterprise security operations.

changes.summary: AI introduces <mark>attack vectors invisible to traditional security</mark> — prompt injection hijacks agent behaviour without triggering a single alert.
key_shifts: AI threat model required: prompt injection, model extraction, data poisoning, adversarial inputs
Zero Trust applied to agents: per-session auth, minimum privilege, time-limited tokens
Agent-to-agent communication needs mutual authentication — trust no agent by default
Semantic attack detection required; traditional WAF and SIEM miss AI-specific threats entirely
what_breaks: Perimeter security models cannot detect attacks that arrive as valid natural language
Enterprise IAM with standing credentials is incompatible with agent Zero Trust requirements
Security teams without AI expertise cannot threat-model systems they do not understand
Supply chain security models have no framework for third-party model API risk
what_survives: Defence-in-depth principles apply — agents need layered controls just as traditional systems do
Incident response structure (detect, contain, eradicate, recover) transfers to AI-specific playbooks
The practice of mapping threats to controls and verifying coverage remains essential
Cross-cutting security architecture integrated across development phases is more critical than ever

future_state.summary: <mark>Zero Trust agent security with semantic attack detection</mark> — every session authenticated, scoped, and monitored for meaning-level threats.
future_state.description: Every agent operates under Zero Trust with per-session authentication, minimum-privilege access, and time-limited tokens. Semantic attack detection supplements traditional SIEM to catch prompt injection, model extraction, and adversarial inputs.
key_practices: Develop an AI threat model mapping prompt injection, model extraction, data poisoning, and adversarial inputs to controls
Enforce per-session authentication and minimum-privilege access for all production agents
Integrate AI-specific security monitoring into the SOC with playbooks for semantic attack categories
Establish supply chain security assessment for third-party model APIs including data provenance requirements

examples.relevance: JPMorgan's $20B technology budget includes zero-trust architecture for AI agents — per-session authentication, minimum privilege, and time-limited tokens for every agent interaction.

Novartis AI Innovation Lab operates under GxP validation requirements where AI security extends beyond data protection to include model integrity, training data provenance, and audit trail completeness.

traditional.summary: Architecture compliance uses graduated conformance levels and structured review at project milestones.
traditional.body_html:
Risk Category

Compliance risk — the risk that implementations deviate from architecture standards, creating technical debt, security exposure, or regulatory violation.

Assessment

Reviews at project initiation, initial design, and major changes. Graduated conformance levels: Irrelevant, Consistent, Compliant, Conformant, Fully Conformant. Structured checklists covering hardware, middleware, applications, information management, security, and system management.

Controls

Formal dispensation (waiver) process for approved deviations with documented justification. Compliance assessments stored in the Governance Repository.

questions.text: Has your organisation extended its architecture compliance checklists with AI-specific sections?

levels.1: No AI-specific items in compliance checklists; AI systems reviewed against the same criteria as traditional systems
levels.2: Some AI-specific items informally considered during reviews but not formally documented in checklists
levels.3: Formal AI-specific checklist sections defined for model governance, agent behaviour, data sovereignty, and AI ethics
levels.4: AI compliance checklists are automated and integrated into deployment pipelines; compliance-as-code enforced for major requirements
levels.5: Continuous automated compliance monitoring across the full AI system lifecycle with real-time dashboards and automated alerting on compliance drift

questions.text: How effectively does your compliance process handle AI systems that can drift out of compliance without code changes?

levels.1: Compliance is assessed only at project milestones; no monitoring between reviews
levels.2: The risk of drift-based non-compliance is acknowledged; some manual periodic checks exist
levels.3: Scheduled compliance re-assessments are conducted for high-risk AI systems between project milestones
levels.4: Continuous compliance monitoring detects model drift, data distribution changes, and regulatory updates with automated alerting
levels.5: Adaptive compliance where regulatory changes trigger automated impact assessment and control adjustment; AI systems self-document compliance evidence

actions.text: Add AI-specific sections to your architecture compliance checklists: model governance, agent behaviour, data sovereignty, and AI ethics. Review against these sections for all current AI deployments.
Implement scheduled compliance re-assessments for deployed AI systems between project milestones, focusing on model drift, data distribution changes, and evolving regulatory requirements.
Pilot compliance-as-code for one high-risk AI system, automating compliance checks within the deployment pipeline and establishing continuous monitoring for post-deployment drift.

changes.summary: AI systems <mark>drift out of compliance without any code change</mark> — milestone reviews catch this months too late.
key_shifts: Continuous compliance monitoring replaces milestone reviews for AI systems
AI-specific checklist sections added: model governance, bias monitoring, data sovereignty, explainability
Dispensation process must be lighter-weight — AI best practices change monthly
Graduated conformance levels map directly to AI Act risk classification tiers
what_breaks: Milestone-based reviews miss model drift, data distribution shifts, and regulatory changes between checkpoints
Compliance ownership models fail when drift crosses team boundaries without a code deployment
Heavy dispensation processes cannot keep pace with monthly shifts in AI best practices
Standard checklists covering hardware, middleware, and applications have no AI-specific items
what_survives: Graduated conformance levels (Irrelevant through Fully Conformant) map naturally to AI risk tiers
Formal dispensation with documented justification remains necessary for approved deviations
Structured compliance checklists are the right mechanism — they just need AI-specific sections
Governance Repository as the compliance record system applies to AI compliance evidence

future_state.summary: <mark>Compliance-as-code with continuous drift detection</mark> — AI systems monitored between milestones and self-documenting conformance.
future_state.description: Compliance is continuously monitored through automated checks that detect model drift, data distribution changes, and regulatory updates. graduated conformance levels are mapped to AI Act risk tiers, and high-risk systems run compliance-as-code in the deployment pipeline.
key_practices: Map every AI system to a graduated conformance level aligned with its AI Act risk classification
Implement continuous compliance monitoring for high-risk AI systems that detects drift between milestones
Pilot compliance-as-code for one high-risk system, automating checks within the deployment pipeline
Create a lightweight dispensation process for AI-specific deviations with quarterly expiry and re-evaluation

traditional.summary: The traditional threat model covers external attackers, insider threats, system vulnerabilities, and data breaches.
traditional.body_html:
Risk Category

External and internal threats to enterprise systems — the conventional threat landscape that security architecture is designed to address.

Assessment

Threat identification through structured analysis, mapping threats to assets, evaluating likelihood and impact, and identifying control gaps. Integrated with industry security standards.

Controls

Defence-in-depth: perimeter security, access controls, encryption, network segmentation, monitoring, and incident response. All assume deterministic systems with known input/output behaviour.

questions.text: Does your organisation have documented threat models that cover AI-specific attack vectors (prompt injection, model extraction, data poisoning, adversarial attacks)?

levels.1: No awareness or documentation of AI-specific threat vectors
levels.2: AI-specific threats are informally understood by some team members but not documented in formal threat models
levels.3: AI-specific threat models are documented with identified attack vectors, risk ratings, and mapped controls for each category
levels.4: Threat models are actively maintained, regularly tested through red-team exercises, and integrated with enterprise security operations
levels.5: Continuous AI threat intelligence feeds inform proactive defence; automated red-teaming validates defences; threat models evolve based on observed attack patterns

questions.text: How effectively does your organisation defend against prompt injection attacks across deployed AI agents?

levels.1: No prompt injection defences in place; agents process all inputs without filtering or validation
levels.2: Basic input filtering exists for some agents but indirect injection vectors are not addressed
levels.3: Systematic prompt injection defences deployed across all production agents, covering both direct and indirect injection vectors
levels.4: Automated prompt injection detection with real-time monitoring and alerting; regular testing of defences against evolving attack techniques
levels.5: Adaptive prompt injection defence that learns from attempted attacks; layered defences including input validation, output verification, and behavioural anomaly detection

actions.text: Catalogue all deployed AI agents and assess each for exposure to the four primary AI threat categories: prompt injection, model extraction, data poisoning, and adversarial inputs.
Implement prompt injection defences for all production agents, addressing both direct injection (malicious user input) and indirect injection (malicious content in processed data sources).
Establish an AI-specific red-team capability that regularly tests deployed agents against evolving AI attack techniques and feeds findings back into threat models.

changes.summary: The most dangerous AI threat is <mark>an agent that is confidently wrong</mark> — it passes every health check while causing business harm.
key_shifts: Semantic monitoring evaluates output correctness — prerequisite for all other AI threat controls
Agent misconduct categories defined: confident errors, scope violations, value misalignment
Supply chain security extends to training data provenance and third-party model integrity
Threat models must include agent-to-agent vectors — agents attacking or misleading other agents
what_breaks: System health monitoring (uptime, error rates, latency) cannot detect confidently wrong outputs
SOC playbooks have no escalation path for agent misconduct that produces successful HTTP responses
Supply chain risk models cannot assess training data provenance for third-party models
Traditional threat classification has no category for emergent misbehaviour in multi-agent systems
what_survives: Structured threat identification and control mapping remains the right analytical approach
Defence-in-depth layering applies to AI threats — no single control is sufficient
Integration with industry security standards provides a foundation to extend for AI threats
Likelihood-and-impact thinking still applies, but likelihood assessment methods must change

future_state.summary: <mark>Semantic monitoring as the security foundation</mark> — every customer-facing and decision-making agent has output correctness evaluation.
future_state.description: Semantic monitoring — evaluating whether agent output is correct and appropriate — is deployed for all customer-facing and decision-making agents. Agent misconduct is classified, monitored, and escalated through playbooks purpose-built for threats that produce no system errors.
key_practices: Deploy semantic monitoring for customer-facing and decision-making agents first — highest risk, highest value
Create agent misconduct classification with response playbooks for confident errors, scope violations, misalignment
Establish supply chain security assessment for third-party model providers including data provenance verification
Conduct regular red-team exercises specifically targeting AI threat vectors across deployed agent chains

traditional.summary: Traditional risk management assumes deterministic systems where failure modes are predictable from component specs.
traditional.body_html:
Risk Category

System failure risk — the risk that architecture components fail to perform as specified, with impacts assessable from component specifications and interaction patterns.

Assessment

Component-level risk analysis: each component examined for failure modes, probability, and impact. Interaction risks assessed from integration specifications and known dependencies.

Controls

Redundancy, failover, error handling, monitoring, and escalation procedures. All assume failures are detectable through system metrics (errors, timeouts, resource exhaustion).

questions.text: Has your organisation implemented blast-radius controls that limit the scope of damage from any single agent failure?

levels.1: No blast-radius controls; agents can access and affect systems without defined boundaries
levels.2: Blast-radius limits are informally defined for some critical agents but not technically enforced
levels.3: Blast-radius controls are defined and enforced for all production agents: data access limits, action scope limits, financial authority caps, and affected system boundaries
levels.4: Automated blast-radius enforcement with real-time monitoring of scope violations; cascade failure scenarios analysed for all multi-agent deployments
levels.5: Self-adjusting blast-radius controls that tighten automatically based on risk signals; predictive cascade failure detection identifies emerging risks before they manifest

questions.text: How does your organisation detect and respond to agent cascade failures where errors propagate through agent chains?

levels.1: No cascade failure detection; agent failures detected only through downstream business impact
levels.2: Basic awareness of cascade risk; some manual monitoring of critical agent chains
levels.3: Distributed tracing across agent interactions enables identification of error propagation paths; circuit breakers implemented for critical agent chains
levels.4: Real-time cascade failure detection with automated containment; semantic drift detection supplements syntactic monitoring; incident forensics can reconstruct full interaction chains
levels.5: Predictive models anticipate cascade failures based on early warning signals; self-healing agent networks with automated isolation and recovery; continuous learning from near-misses

questions.text: Can your organisation detect when an AI agent is producing confidently wrong outputs that appear correct to traditional monitoring?

levels.1: Only system-level monitoring (uptime, error rates, latency); no capability to evaluate output correctness
levels.2: Awareness that agents can be confidently wrong; ad hoc manual spot-checking of outputs for some agents
levels.3: Semantic monitoring deployed for high-risk agents with defined quality metrics: accuracy, consistency, coherence, and scope adherence
levels.4: Systematic semantic monitoring across all production agents with automated anomaly detection and alerting on output quality degradation
levels.5: Continuous output verification with ground-truth validation where available; agents that cross-check each other's outputs; automated escalation to human oversight for anomalous patterns

actions.text: Define blast-radius controls for every deployed agent: what data it can access, what actions it can take, what financial authority it has, and what downstream systems it can affect. Implement technical enforcement for the highest-risk agents first.
Implement distributed tracing across all multi-agent interactions to enable cascade failure detection and root cause analysis. Deploy circuit breakers for critical agent chains.
Deploy semantic monitoring for all production agents that evaluates output quality against defined metrics, with automated alerting on confidence drops, reasoning anomalies, and scope violations.

changes.summary: One agent error propagates through a chain and <mark>cascade failures are invisible until business damage surfaces</mark> — every API call succeeds.
key_shifts: Cascade failures propagate through shared context, not API errors — invisible to monitoring
Blast-radius controls required: cost circuit breakers, authority caps, scope limiters, rollback
Feedback loop detection needed — agents consuming each other's outputs amplify errors exponentially
Emergent behaviour risk requires scenario analysis mapped to business process impact
what_breaks: Component-level failure analysis misses cascade risk that exists only in agent interaction patterns
Monitoring that relies on error codes and timeouts detects zero cascade failures
Infrastructure redundancy and failover do nothing when the failure is semantically wrong output
Incident response cannot reconstruct root cause without distributed tracing across agent chains
what_survives: Risk assessment at integration points is critical — agent interactions are the new integration points
Redundancy and failover principles extend to agent containment through circuit breakers
Escalation procedures remain necessary but must trigger on semantic signals, not just system alerts
Mapping failure modes to business impact applies directly to agent cascade scenarios

future_state.summary: <mark>Containment-first agent architecture</mark> — every agent has blast-radius limits, cost circuit breakers, and cascade detection before production.
future_state.description: Every production agent operates within defined blast-radius boundaries: data access limits, action scope limits, financial authority caps, and downstream system boundaries. Distributed tracing across agent chains enables cascade failure detection and circuit breakers halt propagation.
key_practices: Define blast-radius controls for every agent: data access scope, action limits, financial authority caps, affected systems
Deploy cost circuit breakers first — simplest control, highest ROI in preventing runaway agent damage
Map agent interaction chains to business processes to identify cascade risk paths proactively
Implement distributed tracing across all multi-agent workflows with semantic drift detection at each hop

traditional.summary: Distributed tracing from microservices tracks request flow across services using request IDs and timing.
traditional.body_html:
Risk Category

Diagnostic risk — the risk of being unable to determine root cause when distributed systems produce incorrect outcomes.

Assessment

Microservices observability provides the closest precedent: distributed tracing tracks request flow, logging captures events, metrics track system health. Failure diagnosis follows the request trace.

Controls

Correlation IDs, structured logging, metrics dashboards, alerting on system health thresholds, and distributed trace analysis for root cause investigation.

questions.text: Does your organisation have distributed tracing that tracks semantic content and reasoning across multi-agent interactions — not just request flow and timing?

levels.1: No agent-specific tracing; standard APM tools track system metrics only
levels.2: Basic agent-specific logging exists for some high-risk agents; confidence scores tracked for individual agents but not across chains
levels.3: Distributed tracing covers all production agent interactions, capturing reasoning traces, confidence scores, input/output summaries, and context mutations
levels.4: Full semantic tracing operational with queryable interaction histories; any business outcome can be traced back through its complete agent interaction chain
levels.5: Observability data feeds automated agent tuning and routing decisions; predictive models identify emerging risks from interaction pattern analysis

questions.text: How mature are your observability dashboards and alerting for agent-specific failure signatures?

levels.1: Standard infrastructure dashboards only; no visibility into agent reasoning or interaction patterns
levels.2: Some ad hoc dashboards for individual agent performance; no unified view or agent-specific alerting
levels.3: Unified observability dashboards presenting agent-level and system-level health; alerting rules tuned to agent-specific failure signatures (confidence drops, reasoning anomalies, context corruption)
levels.4: Real-time dashboards with automated anomaly detection across agent interaction patterns; feedback loop and deadlock detection operational
levels.5: Self-learning observability that automatically identifies new failure signatures and adjusts alerting thresholds; near-miss detection feeds continuous improvement

actions.text: Instrument your highest-risk multi-agent workflow with semantic tracing that captures reasoning traces, confidence scores, and context mutations at each step in the agent chain.

Build a unified agent observability dashboard that presents both system-level and semantic-level health indicators, with alerting rules for confidence drops, reasoning anomalies, and interaction pattern deviations.
Establish incident forensics protocols that can reconstruct full agent interaction chains, including context states and decision points, for any agent-involved incident.

changes.summary: Without <mark>semantic-level tracing</mark>, you cannot answer "why did the agent do that?" when a customer complains or a regulator asks.
key_shifts: Semantic tracing captures meaning and correctness, not just request flow and timing
Causal chain tracking across multi-agent interactions enables root cause analysis
Traces must be queryable: "show every decision influenced by this data source"
Agent observability is build-not-buy today — no major APM vendor supports it natively
what_breaks: Traditional APM tracking request flow and latency reveals nothing about output correctness
Incident forensics cannot reconstruct agent decision chains without semantic trace data
Regulatory accountability is impossible to demonstrate without causal provenance records
Teams that attempt full observability without phasing deliver nothing useful
what_survives: Correlation IDs and distributed tracing patterns extend naturally to agent interaction chains
Structured logging discipline applies — agents just need richer semantic payloads
Alerting on threshold breaches transfers to agent-specific failure signatures
The principle of instrumenting before optimising is more important than ever with agents

future_state.summary: <mark>Full semantic tracing across agent chains</mark> — every business outcome traceable through the complete reasoning path that produced it.
future_state.description: Every agent logs inputs, outputs, model version, and reasoning trace. Distributed tracing links multi-agent interactions into queryable causal chains, enabling both real-time anomaly detection and post-incident forensic reconstruction.
key_practices: Start with minimum viable observability: log every agent's inputs, outputs, and model version — 80% of forensic value
Add distributed tracing across agent chains with causal linking before investing in semantic analysis
Build queryable interaction histories so any business outcome can be traced to its full reasoning chain
Establish incident forensics protocols that reconstruct agent decision chains including context and model versions

traditional.togaf_reference: FC Part 5, Ch 6; TSG Risk and Security
traditional.summary: Traditional EA maps architecture compliance to industry standards and provides compliance review processes.
traditional.body_html:
Risk Category

Regulatory compliance risk — the risk that deployed systems violate applicable laws, regulations, and industry standards.

Assessment

Compliance assessed through architecture reviews mapped to external standards. The TSG Risk and Security guide maps EA to security frameworks. Compliance is verified at project milestones using structured checklists.

Controls

Architecture standards aligned to regulatory requirements. Compliance reviews at milestones. Dispensation process for approved deviations. Governance repository maintains compliance records.

questions.text: Has your organisation inventoried and classified all deployed AI systems against applicable AI-specific regulations (EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, ISO 42001)?

levels.1: No inventory of AI systems against regulatory requirements; compliance addressed reactively if at all
levels.2: AI systems are partially inventoried; some awareness of applicable regulations but no systematic classification
levels.3: All AI systems inventoried and classified by regulatory risk level; documentation requirements identified per system; human oversight mechanisms in place for high-risk systems
levels.4: Comprehensive algorithmic compliance framework operational with automated classification, monitoring, and reporting; bias detection implemented for high-risk systems
levels.5: Adaptive compliance where regulatory changes trigger automated impact assessment and control adjustment; AI systems self-document compliance evidence across their lifecycle

questions.text: How does your organisation track and respond to changes in AI-specific regulatory requirements across relevant jurisdictions?

levels.1: No systematic tracking of AI regulatory changes; compliance gaps discovered reactively
levels.2: Informal awareness of major regulatory developments; no structured process for assessing impact on deployed AI systems
levels.3: Regulatory change tracking process established with impact assessment for new or evolving AI regulations; compliance roadmap maintained
levels.4: Proactive regulatory change management with automated impact assessment; compliance-as-code implemented for major regulatory requirements
levels.5: Regulatory intelligence feeds inform architecture planning; automated compliance adjustment when regulations change; active participation in regulatory development processes

questions.text: To what extent has your organisation implemented transparency and explainability capabilities for AI systems that require them?

levels.1: No transparency or explainability capabilities for AI systems
levels.2: Basic documentation exists for some AI systems but no systematic explainability architecture
levels.3: Explainability requirements defined per AI system risk classification; transparency mechanisms implemented for high-risk systems; decision audit trails maintained
levels.4: Automated explainability generation for all regulated AI systems; decision provenance tracked end-to-end; transparency reports generated on demand
levels.5: Real-time explainability integrated into all AI system outputs; stakeholder-appropriate explanations generated automatically; continuous validation of explanation accuracy

actions.text: Inventory all deployed AI systems and classify each against applicable regulations: EU AI Act risk classification, NIST AI RMF applicability, ISO 42001 scope, and GDPR automated decision-making obligations.
Establish a regulatory change tracking process for AI-specific regulations with systematic impact assessment for each change against your deployed and planned AI systems.

Implement explainability and transparency mechanisms for all high-risk AI systems, including decision audit trails, automated documentation, and human oversight mechanisms required by applicable regulations.

Develop a unified algorithmic compliance framework synthesising requirements from EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, ISO 42001, and GDPR into organisation-specific policies, with compliance-as-code automation for major requirements.

changes.summary: EU AI Act fines reach 7% of global turnover — <mark>most organisations have no AI compliance posture</mark> and regulations are tightening.
key_shifts: Every AI system must be classified by regulatory risk tier (EU AI Act, GDPR, sector-specific)
Continuous compliance monitoring replaces milestone reviews — model drift creates violations silently
Explainability must serve multiple audiences: regulators, customers, and internal technical teams
Regulatory change management is now a standing capability — the landscape shifts quarterly
what_breaks: Milestone-based compliance verification misses violations from model drift between reviews
Organisations without an AI system inventory cannot comply with regulations for uncatalogued systems
Single-audience explainability fails regulators, customers, and technical teams needing different detail levels
Static regulatory mapping cannot track AI-specific regulations shifting across jurisdictions quarterly
what_survives: Architecture standards aligned to regulatory requirements remain the right governance mechanism
Structured compliance reviews with checklists extend to AI-specific regulations
The dispensation process for approved deviations applies to AI regulatory requirements
Governance Repository as the compliance evidence store is essential for AI audit trails

future_state.summary: <mark>Unified algorithmic compliance framework</mark> synthesising EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, ISO 42001, and GDPR into automated, monitored controls.
future_state.description: Every AI system is inventoried, classified by regulatory risk tier, and monitored continuously for compliance drift. Explainability is architected for multiple audiences with automated evidence generation across the system lifecycle.
key_practices: Inventory and classify all deployed AI systems against EU AI Act risk tiers and GDPR automated decision-making rules
Fix GDPR automated decision-making violations first — that is already law with active enforcement
Build multi-audience explainability: regulator audit trails, customer explanations, and technical decision attribution
Establish a standing regulatory change management function assessing AI regulation changes quarterly
